verdicts.interest_take: The show interrogating its own most famous prop, relocating belief from wall to person — the app's own thesis.
verdicts.writing_notes: Four fields distinct; 'idea' is the real payoff and lands the heart/head/gut move without dialogue. Warm, clear.

verdicts.interest_take: A one-line key that reads the title two ways at once (signage and omens) — genuinely orienting, not recap.
verdicts.writing_notes: Honest that 'fate vs. free will' is a gloss; concise. Title category fit good. Pairs naturally before the omen cards.

verdicts.interest_take: A garbled compliment landing word-for-word as prophecy — the funniest, most 'I never caught that' of the signs.
verdicts.writing_notes: Tight; surprise carries it. Avoids reproducing the exact phrase. Relationships category is a slight stretch but defensible.

verdicts.interest_take: The mercurial guru vanishes the day after insisting he wasn't needed — a clean gut-punch that rhymes with Ted's speech.
verdicts.writing_notes: Self-contained, funny, well-paced. 'idea' ties it to the episode's belief theme rather than just recapping the exit.

verdicts.interest_take: A redemptive flicker mid-villain-arc: the sincere Nate outshines the borrowed bravado. Easy to undervalue on first watch.
verdicts.writing_notes: Clean contrast in 'idea'; warm. Redemption category right. Distinct from every other card in the set.

verdicts.interest_take: A prediction she could shrug off becomes a physical object already in her hand — the concrete hinge of the omen arc.
verdicts.writing_notes: Strong 'idea'; 'why' flags the rewatch payoff honestly. Slight overlap with the malapropism card but weightier and distinct.

verdicts.interest_take: Ted managing anxiety in real time — quiet evidence the S2 work is holding. Insightful but lower-surprise.
verdicts.writing_notes: Handles a delicate beat with the right calm tone. 'why' is upfront that it leans on the S2 storyline.

verdicts.interest_take: Tender, but the weakest of the set: it's the omen thread turning serious, already covered by the matchbook/framing cards.
verdicts.writing_notes: Grounding only 3 (barely clears the gate) and interest 3; the fourth 'signs'-adjacent card thins the set's variety. Drops as motherhood thread is forward-looking with little self-contained payoff here.

episode_notes: Strong, well-organized set anchored by the BELIEVE-sign card, which is the literal heart of this app. The episode's gift is its tight thematic braid — the title 'Signs' works on the banner and on Rebecca's omens at once — so the framing card plus two payoff cards (matchbook, malapropism) earn their places by reading as a deliberate trio rather than recap. One real risk: clustering. FOUR included cards orbited the omen/signs thread (framing, matchbook, malapropism, motherhood). I cut s3e5-rebecca-motherhood-doctor to protect variety and best-first feel: it has the set's lowest scores (grounding 3, interest 3), is forward-looking, and its payoff lands later in S3, so it adds weight without adding a new flavor. That leaves seven winners spanning belief, comedy, redemption, and mental health — good spread for a warm rewatch. Ordering: led with the thematic anchor and the title key, then the two funniest/most-surprising beats (malapropism, Zava) and Nate's redemption to keep momentum, with the more spoiler-dependent matchbook and Ted's panic attack (forward/series) last. The grounding-reviewer correctly merged the production 'meta' card into the philosophy one and correctly held the contested Steinem/Biblical 'truth will set you free' attribution; no excluded card warrants rescue — the two unverified ones (Trent's book title, the Future Islands needle-drop) belong to the S3 finale, not this episode.
